`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 61 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content: Let me check if the dot moved this time. I clicked on (28, 26) which is the top-right cell of the dot.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Magenta dots:")
for n in seg['nodes']:

        print(f"  id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The dot moved from (28, 25) to (27, 25)! So it moved up by 1 but stayed in the same column. Previously, when I clicked on the top-left cell (28, 25), it moved up-left (to 27, 24). But when I clicked on the top-right cell (28, 26), it moved up (to 27, 25).
Wait, let me re-examine. The dot was at boundary [[28, 25], [28, 26], [29, 26], [29, 25]], which means cells (28,25), (28,26), (29,25), (29,26).
After clicking on (28, 26), the dot is at boundary [[27, 25], [27, 26], [28, 26], [28, 25]], which means cells (27,25), (27,26), (28,25), (28,26).
So the dot moved up by 1 row (from rows 28-29 to rows 27-28), but stayed in the same columns (25-26).
This is different from before! Previously, when I clicked on the top-left cell, the dot moved up-left (both row and column decreased). But when I clicked on the top-right cell, the dot only moved up (row decreased, column stayed same).
So the direction of movement depends on which cell of the 2x2 dot I click:
- Click TL (top-left) -> move up-left
- Click TR (top-right) -> move up
- Click BL (bottom-left) -> ?
- Click BR (bottom-right) -> ?
This is a crucial discovery! The dots can move in different directions depending on which cell I click. Let me test the other two cells to confirm.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The dot is now at (27, 25). Let me check:
- Previous position: (28, 25) -> cells (28,25), (28,26), (29,25), (29,26)
- Clicked on (28, 26) which is TR
- New position: (27, 25) -> cells (27,25), (27,26), (28,25), (28,26)
So the dot moved up by 1, staying in the same columns. This is consistent with clicking on the right side of the dot making it move up (not up-left).
Let me test clicking on the left side to see if it moves left, and clicking on the bottom to see if it moves down.
Actually, I need to be careful. The previous clicks on the dot were:
1. Click on (29, 26) when dot was at (28, 25) -> dot stayed at (28, 25)
2. Click on (28, 26) when dot was at (28, 25) -> dot moved to (27, 25)
Wait, in case 1, the dot was at (28, 25) with cells (28,25), (28,26), (29,25), (29,26). I clicked on (29, 26) which is BR. The dot stayed at (28, 25).
In case 2, the dot was at (28, 25) with cells (28,25), (28,26), (29,25), (29,26). I clicked on (28, 26) which is TR. The dot moved to (27, 25).
So:
- Click BR -> no movement (or maybe move down-right, but blocked?)
- Click TR -> move up
Let me test clicking on BL and TL to see the full pattern.
Actually, I realize I need to be more systematic. Let me test all 4 corners of the current dot position (27, 25):
- TL: (27, 25)
- TR: (27, 26)
- BL: (28, 25)
- BR: (28, 26)

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me test all 4 corners of the dot at (27, 25)
# Cells: (27,25)=TL, (27,26)=TR, (28,25)=BL, (28,26)=BR
# I'll click on each and see where the dot moves

# First, let me record the current position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
dot_pos = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4 and n['id'] == 9:
        dot_pos = n['boundary']
        break
print(f"Dot 9 current: {dot_pos}")

# Test TL (27, 25)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 25}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4 and n['id'] == 9:
        print(f"After TL click: {n['boundary']}")
        break

# Test TR (27, 26) - but dot moved, need to recalculate
# Let me just do one test at a time
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
